You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Level 1 of a grid puzzle game. The board shows a gray structure with various colored elements. Need to understand the layout and goal before acting.
- Plan: Inspect the current frame segmentation to understand objects and their relationships, then determine the best action.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after the RIGHT action. I need to compare the before and after frames to see if the player moved or if something else changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after RIGHT action
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation

# Compare node positions - look for movement
before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before_seg['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after_seg['nodes']}

print("=== Changes after RIGHT ===")
for nid in after_nodes:
    bn = before_nodes.get(nid)
    an = after_nodes[nid]
    if bn and bn['boundary'] != an['boundary']:
        print(f"  Node {nid} (color={an['color']}) moved:")
        print(f"    before: {bn['boundary']}")
        print(f"    after:  {an['boundary']}")

# Check if any nodes appeared/disappeared
before_ids = set(before_nodes.keys())
after_ids = set(after_nodes.keys())
print(f"\n  New nodes: {after_ids - before_ids}")
print(f"  Removed nodes: {before_ids - after_ids}")

# Check the result
print(f"\n  Result: {last_action_result}")

# Check bottom bar (timer) changes
print("\n=== Bottom bar area ===")
ascii_after = after.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(59, 64):
    print(f"  {r}: {ascii_after[r][10:63]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
From the changes after RIGHT:
- Node 10 (orange) moved right from col 34-38 to col 39-43
- Node 11 (blue) moved right from col 34-38 to col 39-43
- Node 2 (gray structure) boundary changed - the right side shifted
- Node 16 (yellow bar) shrunk - timer bar
- Nodes 17, 18, 19 (red squares) shifted left - these seem to be part of a HUD
- New node 20 appeared
The orange and blue blocks at the bottom of the gray structure moved right. This suggests the player controls those blocks. The yellow bar at the bottom is a timer that's shrinking.
Let me look at the bottom-left area where the blue L-shape (id=13) is - that might be the actual player avatar. Or the orange/blue blocks might be what I control.
Actually, looking more carefully at the image, I see:
- A gray maze structure in the center
- A white cross/plus sign inside the gray area (target?)
- Orange and blue blocks at the bottom of the gray area (these moved right)
- A black box at top with blue elements
- A black box at bottom-left with blue elements (this might be the player)
The player avatar is likely the blue L-shape in the bottom-left black box (id=13). The orange/blue blocks might be something else.
Wait, but the orange (id=10) and blue (id=11) blocks moved when I pressed RIGHT. So those are what I control. The goal might be to navigate through the gray maze to reach the white cross target.
Let me look at the maze more carefully. The gray structure has:
- A dark charcoal obstacle (id=6) inside it
- The white cross target (id=7,8,9) at around row 31-33, col 20-22
- The orange/blue blocks at row 45-49, col 34-38 (now 39-43 after moving right)
The orange bar is 4 pixels wide and 2 tall. The blue bar is 4 wide and 3 tall. They seem to be stacked.
I think the player is the orange/blue block combination at the bottom of the gray structure. The goal is probably to navigate to the white cross target.
But wait - the blocks are at the bottom of the gray structure and the target is higher up and to the left. I need to figure out the maze layout.
Let me look at the ASCII more carefully around the gray structure to understand the maze paths. The charcoal area (id=6) seems to be an obstacle.
Let me try moving UP to see if I can go up through the maze.

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The orange/blue blocks moved right. They're the player.
# Target is the white cross at ~row 31-33, col 20-22
# Let me check the maze layout more carefully

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Print the full gray structure area to see paths
print("=== Full maze area (rows 24-50, cols 14-54) ===")
for r in range(24, 51):
    if r < len(ascii_lines):
        print(f"{r:2d}: {ascii_lines[r][14:54]}")

# The player (orange+blue) is now at col 39-43
# Target (white cross) is at col 20-22, row 31-33
# Need to navigate through the maze
# Let me try UP to see if there's a path up
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
